title: Two Outs — Get the Easy One!

root.narration: Two outs, runners on first and second. The batter hits a ground ball right at you -- you're the second baseman. You field it cleanly on one hop. The runner from second is heading to third, the runner from first is heading to second, and the batter is sprinting to first. You have the ball. Three runners are moving. With two outs, where do you throw to get out of the inning?

choices.text: Throw to first base! It's the closest and easiest force out!
Throw to third to get the lead runner!
Chase down the runner going to second and tag them!

outcome-first-great.narration: You turn and fire to first base. The first baseman stretches and catches it -- the batter is OUT! THIRD OUT! Inning over! It doesn't matter that the other runners reached their bases because three outs ends the inning. No runs score! You picked the easiest, shortest throw and ended the inning. Smart play!

outcome.headline: Inning Over! Smart Throw to First!
outcome.explanation: With two outs, you only need ONE more out to end the inning. It doesn't matter which runner you get -- ANY out will do. So always go for the EASIEST one! First base was the best choice because: (1) it's a force out, so the first baseman just has to catch the ball while touching the bag, (2) the throw is short from your second base position, and (3) the batter is the slowest to reach their base since they just started running. Getting fancy with a longer throw to third risks an error that could let runs score. With two outs, take the sure thing!
outcome.whatToRemember: With two outs, get the EASIEST out -- usually at first base. You only need one more out to end the inning. Don't try to be a hero with a tough throw!

keyTerms.definition: When a runner must advance to the next base because the batter is running behind them. The fielder just has to touch the base while holding the ball -- no tag needed!
The easiest, most reliable out you can get on a play. Smart players go for the sure out instead of trying for a harder, riskier play.
The third out of an inning ends the inning immediately. Any runners who haven't crossed home plate yet do NOT score, even if they're almost there!

outcome-third-okay.narration: You throw to third base to get the lead runner. The third baseman catches it and slaps the tag on the sliding runner -- OUT! Inning over! But that was a risky throw. The runner at third is fast, and your throw had to travel farther across the diamond. If the throw was off or the runner beat the tag, they'd be safe at third and the inning would still be going. It worked this time, but first base was the safer play.

outcome.headline: Out at Third! But Risky!
outcome.explanation: You got the out, so the result is fine. But let's talk about WHY first base is usually better with two outs. At third base, the runner had a head start and was already close to the bag. You needed a TAG play (not a force out), which means the fielder had to catch the ball AND tag the runner. The throw was also longer. At first base, the batter just started running and the first baseman only needs to touch the bag. Shorter throw, force out, slower runner -- that's three reasons first base is the better choice. Always take the sure out!
outcome.whatToRemember: With two outs, go for the EASIEST out, not the fanciest one. First base is usually the safest choice because it's a short throw and a force out.

outcome-chase-bad.narration: You start running after the runner heading to second base! But runners are FAST and you're not going to catch them in a foot race. The runner sees you coming and either slides into second or stops and makes you chase them. Meanwhile, the batter reaches first, the other runner reaches third, and you're stuck in a rundown. By the time you finally tag the runner, the lead runner has scored! The run counts because it crossed the plate before the tag was made.

outcome.headline: Chasing Runners Never Works!
outcome.explanation: Never chase a runner when you have the ball. Runners are fast and they have a head start! While you were chasing one runner, the other runners were advancing. This is a rundown situation, and rundowns give the offense time. The golden rule with two outs is: make the EASY play. You had the ball 15 feet from first base -- a simple toss ends the inning with no runs scoring. Instead, a run scored and the inning could have gotten even worse. Let the ball do the work -- THROW it to a base, don't carry it there!
outcome.whatToRemember: Never chase a runner! Throw the ball to a base for the out. Chasing wastes time and lets other runners advance. Let the ball do the traveling, not your feet!
